Okay, in this session, our final session, Dan is going to reveal what should go into your referral toolkit and how to do wildly effective client referral events and how to get referrals from unlikely sources you probably never thought about before. So Dan, let's start with talking about something that people don't usually do. Actually, let's start with what should be in the referral toolbox. Let's start with there, then we're going to get to some more sophisticated strategies, which is the events. Well, you want to give clients a variety of tools that they can use to bring people to you, refer people to you, refer people to your information. And to a great extent, the more tools, you know, the better. If you have a book that is informational and leads to you, you definitely want to give them two or three or four or six copies. Just like Dr. Brilson did we show it earlier. Exactly. If you have a DVD, a couple of DVDs. If you have a CD, a couple of CDs. If you are driving to a static standardized online presentation, then you certainly give them some cards, something that is pocket sized, glove compartment sized, etc. That drives to that presentation. Seven things every parent needs to know about X. Thanks. Every month you sort of restock them with those lead generation cards, handout pieces, whatever the drive to the report of the month, the online video of the month, and so forth. So, a lot of it is what we went in. I mean, if you just pick the pieces that we put in there, you want some entertainment in there, you talk about things like that, things that are fun. So, how do you train the clients, though, on how to use these? So, the first training is the overriding understanding that if you are a customer, a client, or a patient here, one of the things you do is refer. And we have, we are now giving them tools to do that with. It is helpful if you have a scripted, here's what to do conversation that occurs between you and customer client patient, or between your staff and customer client patient, about a primary means of referring. So, we often have in healthcare, in financial services, there will be the book and the shock and awe package, the literature package put together into some sort of convenient, sort of doesn't fall all over the place. I like clear plastic bags myself. And you might get three of them. Usually, like after your welcome kit, say, well, by the way, you know, clients around here refer and often ask us, so here's all you have to do. Here's three kits. You can keep them in a trunk of your car. You can keep them at home, you can keep it at the office, whatever. And anytime, you know, you have a conversation with somebody who has chronic headaches, has a crap load of money and doesn't know what to do with it, has, you know, whatever. All you got to do is give them one of these and say, here, this is a really interesting book and some information from Billy Bob, who manages our money. He's the guy who manages our money. I think you'll really find this book interesting. That's all you got to do. And if you say it like three times, they memorize it, you know, they get it, right? Some people will give them a manual that literally is, here's how to refer people to us. Right? And here's, so if you look at his map of the box, think of a map. Here's the five tools we gave you. Here's how to use them. Here's how to use them. Right? Some will give them, here's who we want and here's who we don't want. Right. So here's who we want. Here's who we don't want. So the important premise is that this is the opposite of entitlement expectation. This is nothing good is going to happen automatically. We've got to make it happen. Right. And it is the fact that customers, clients and patients can be trained. They can be trained about how they're supposed to behave in every respect, whether you're supposed to be here on time or not, whether you're supposed to, you know, do your, what, and that behavior includes, you're supposed to refer and here's how we do this around here. One of the key things that you didn't say though, which I know is a staff though. Your staff needs to be trained as well. Talk a little bit about that. And by the way, in this, we have an actual script that I believe you wrote for what the staff should say to generate referrals for you. Yeah, it's a direct ask. It's a direct ask script. Yeah, it says direct assets. So let's talk about training because like we talked about it earlier, I think in segment one. Look, if they have, if they are between you and your customer and the referrals that come from your customer, then they've got to be in the game. And in many businesses, they have more opportunity to influence this than the owner or the practitioner anyway. In a dental office, the front desk staff, the hygienists actually have more opportunity to influence this than the doctor does. So as with all sales management, which is what it is, we, you got to have a process. It's got to be taught, coached and forced monitored and forced and rewarded or punished based on compliance. Yeah. So again, and so you can't, you know, you can't let this be random and casual and expect anything but random and casual results. Right. Now you can force these conversations, the button. Ask me. Ask me about headache cures. I mean, you know, you can force it. Signage, you can do a lot of things in a physical environment. But still, that person has to, hey, look, did you get this month's, you know, referral cards for the report? Right. Here, let me give you six of them. Right. I mean, it has to happen. Right. And it just falls in with anything else. If you have an upsell script, if you have whatever they're supposed to be doing, if you're not teaching, coaching, monitoring and enforcing, you're not going to get it. Right. Exactly. So again, I just want to show everybody, so this is in your manual, right? It's called the direct ask. Okay. Direct ask. And there's an actual script. Say that 10 times fast. Yeah, right. Maybe earlier. I mean, need some more sugar to do that. Yes. I'm going to need some more sugar. So, but the direct ask script, again, that's right in your manual. So, you want to make sure that you check that out. And again, you got to train the staff on how to use it. You got to practice. You got to drill. You got to rehearse with them and make sure that they get it done. All right. So, let's move on to something really cool, which I would make a bet that most people aren't doing. But if you do it, it's very powerful, which are client events, client referral events. Talk a little bit about those because, boy. Well, so everybody's seen some version of them, usually not done well. But the factory reps from these five different brands of RVs are going to be here. But so they will publicly advertise these things and they will advertise them to their customers. What they don't do is really make them feel like they're going to be here. So, they will advertise them to their customers. They will advertise them to their customers. What they don't do is really make them about, here's a thing to bring other people to. Right. Right. And they also aren't very good about doing the non-sales activity event for people to bring other people to. So we have a member, for example, who's in a dairy business, Alan Reed. And so Alan has a dairy. He's got cows and milk and machines and stuff. And they have home delivery. So I think he's got 3,000, 3,200, some good-sized number of customers who their trucks deliver milk and ice cream stuff too. And they have a store, a retail store. And so once a year, they have farm days at the farm. And all the customers are encouraged, bring family, bring friends, invite people from out, do whatever you want to do. Our farm is your farm. And for these two days, there's picnic area, there's somebody out there cooking burgers and hot dogs for everybody. There's the ride. There's the go watch the cows get milked. There's the fake cow milk thing the kids do with the fake cow. And there's a competition. And there's all these activities and stuff. And the year I was there, I'm pretty sure over a two-day period of time, over 4,000 people, roughly 50% customer, 50% non-customer, come to this thing. And they're there for three and four and five hours. And they're meeting and greeting staff and they're sampling product. They directly sign people up right there to become customers. But even when they don't. And this kind of event, there's a chiropractor for many years in Florida who used to come to my seminars who had a big piece of property with a lake and everything. And he ran his two-day customer appreciation event very similar to this except no cows. But it was bring everybody you want. And there's boats to go out on the lake on. There's cookout. There's a movie on big screen at night. And he would have 8,000, 9,000 people go through this place. And patients would bring 6, 7, 10, 12 people. People had their class reunions during his thing at his place. And he said, it's all I got to do. It's it. I do that. And I'm done for the year. I can't take any more patients. There was an insurance agent in Phoenix for years. He had his birthday thing. He had a birthday thing and he had a St. Patrick's Day thing. In both times, he just rented out this bar, put up big tents. At his birthday, he sat up on a big throne the whole time and invited customers. But they were supposed to bring everybody you want. And you had to bring a gag gift. And you could donate the charity if you wanted to. But it was free booze, free food. And this thing grew from a few hundred to a thousand to thousands. And he told me the same thing. He said, I really don't have to do any other marketing. He said, this pretty much gives me all the client referrals I could ever possibly want and I'm busy. In B2B, sort of summer's white, same thing with an annual dinner before the National Speaker Association convention. So there's the, you know, implant seminar. There's the cosmetic surgery seminar. There's the how not to have back pain class that you encourage customers, clients, patients to bring people to. If you're really aggressive, we always talk to chiropractors. One of the parts of becoming a new patient was mandatory attendance at the back care class within two weeks of being a patient. And you had to bring a buddy. Oh, wow. Wow. And you didn't, you got fired as a patient. So we forced it. So there's that category of activity. Then there's this other category of activity that is more about just letting people be introduced to you. In a fun, in a fun and, you know, not salesy, not, you know. Yeah, because that's how you ruin it, right? With one of those events, right? I created for the financial advisors, I created to do it right around Father's Day, baseball night in the office, if you got a big enough office. Put a big screen TV in, get some DVDs of old baseball movies and pick clips and get some hot dogs and popcorn and beer and no pitch, no stand up, no do nothing. Just all the clients bring your kids and bring your buddies to baseball night. And I got my literature over here. I got copies of my books over here. If anybody wants them, fine. If you got a money question and you want to come ask me, feel free and let's have a good time, right? One of the things, again, this is a little bit advanced stuff that we're talking about here in the final session, but you talk about how that all referrals don't come just from clients. I think when people think of referrals, they think of coming from clients, customers, or patients. You talk about phishing upstream to get referrals. What does that mean? Well, so somebody has your customer or has your client or has your patient now has a customer in a non-competitive environment. They have a whole bunch of them because customers of a feather flock together. The first thing to think about is how can I somehow make you want to tell your customers about me? You don't have to be my customer necessarily for that to happen. So most professions have peer-to-peer referrals. They're sort of familiar with that. So lawyers will get referrals from other attorneys. And the guy who specializes in DUI will get a referral from a business attorney who doesn't handle DUI, but his client has now gotten picked up, driving drunk. So everybody's familiar with that, but that's pretty narrow thinking. So you can be much broader in cultivating these relationships. Ben Glass, who runs a personal injury law practice, remember ours is very smart about it for his practice. So he does a couple of things. One, he actually runs a local mixed breed of dog business mastermind group, really his own networking group, where he brings other kinds of business people together. Typically ones who have families as customers, what he is client, because somebody's going to have an accident and they're going to tell somebody about it. He does his online TV show and has you on as a guest and gives you a copy of it that you can go use yourself and promotes you, you, the local high-end furniture store, who you notify all your customers. So a lot of this can get forced by sort of paying it forward, helping other people who then in turn let you into their list. A lot of times you can do it with content if you are prolific with content. And I'm in the content business, but you don't have to be in the content business. Naturally Ben can provide all sorts of content about what you need to know if you're in an accident, what you need to know to hire an attorney, what you need to know to fire an attorney, and financial planners can use that in their newsletters. They can use that on their websites. Insurance people can use that in their newsletters and use it on their website. So you can be a content pusher to all sorts of other businesses who need content. If I was local, I would be very alert to any and every other kind of business putting out their own customer newsletter because they're lazy and they need content. I'll provide them content. I do that nationally. And so I'm happy to have books excerpted. We provide articles. We provide fake interviews where I give you a four-page interview about a topic or about my latest book. And here's all the answers and here's the holes for the questions, write your questions and feel free to use it. All that creates this upstream downstream movement of your customers seeing me here, here, here, here, the smart ones say to themselves at some point, gee, he's here, here, here, here, here. Maybe I should swim upstream from those places and take a look at him. And again, online, this is ultra simple. You guess blog posts, guess newsletter articles online, all of that. I mean, it's really simple to do. In your manual, there is an actual referral letter as well that you can take and that you can use to send out. Dan, talk a little bit about the endorse mailing. Well, the endorse mailing is a very direct way to do this. And sometimes it requires reciprocity, in which case you have to think about whether or not you're willing to do that. Sometimes it doesn't. It is often done through a customer, client or patient. So you have to be alert to customers, clients or patients who are centers of influence, who have some population of people more than an ordinary customer has a dozen close neighbors, friends, and coworkers, and has maybe a total universe of 50 or so. There's centers of influence who have hundreds and hundreds and hundreds who know them. So the example that's in the manual, at least one of the examples that's in the manual, is of the real estate agent. So I think I don't think I have it. I put it away because it was in the manual. But so the example in there is of a dentist getting a real estate agent to do the endorsed mailing. And so the real estate agent who is the patient of the dentist has two really great centers of influence. So there you go. Thank you. So one is his clients. So your fiance, not now, but will eventually have hundreds and hundreds of clients that she has found a house for or sold a house for. Certainly the ones she's found the house for are still in the area and she'll be maintaining contact with them. So she has that circle of influence. The other circle of influence she has is all the other real estate agents, peer to peer. And so there's two versions of the same endorsed mailing letter in here, which is for my valued clients, why is a realtor writing to you about a dentist? Dear friend, and then here's why. And this drives, by the way, to a special report. So it doesn't drive to the dentist. I'm not suggesting you hurry into my dentist. You probably have one. Maybe like me, you've been postponing and procrastinating over some needed care, regardless with no obligation of any kind. Dr. Gummybure will be happy to send you copies of his book on dental health title and his special report, How to Look 10 Years Younger and 10 Pounds Thinner, and the information kit about his practice. You know, I've been teaching this in endorsed mailing for years and this is the first time I've heard having it go to the report. Well, it makes it easier to get him to do it. Well, then you put him into your lead generation funnel and it takes over. And then the version of it that goes to the customer is nearly the same, or the agents, for my fellow agents. Why is a realtor writing to you about a dentist? And there's some reason, you know, at the top. That's one is you and I know what being of service is all about. Every once in a while, all two seldom, you get such exceptional service from somebody, that you just have to brag about them. But the drive is to the report. The drive is not to go in and get your teeth pried on. Brilliant. And so when you drive the information, it's easier to get somebody to agree to do it. Sure. That it is if you're driving direct to the practice. Number one. Number two, you'll get more response. Right. And all of this is, as an aside, it's sort of forcing you to be in the lead generation, lead development, multi-step marketing arena, rather than in the one step we only get by now customers where most people live. Right. You talk a lot about, and again, this is in here for you to model. So you talk about omnipresence and you mentioned it a couple of times, but let's sum it up. What exactly is omnipresence? You're there in front of your customer or you're developing prospects, but for the sake of referrals in front of your customer. So that they can't go a day, the goal, they can't go a day and they can't turn it in a 360 degree circle without bumping into you. That's the goal. So if you think about somebody who is a Amazon Prime customer, they buy more and more and more stuff from Amazon and less and less and less stuff from every place else. So pretty soon, they can't really turn a full circle around in their house without seeing stuff they bought from Amazon, which reminds them, oh shit, I can buy this from Amazon. I don't got to go to a store, I don't got to, hey, I can get diapers, I can get whatever, Amazon shipping, elephants and elephant manure and everything in between. And they think about it a lot because Amazon communicates with them a lot. If you think about Disney, a Disney fan, you cannot be in their house. They will not turn around 360 degrees and not bump into Disney. And Disney is communicating with them every single damn day. Broad in general, turn on the TV and click one night and try not to bump into a Disney movie, a Disney show, a Disney ad, a Disney something. They integrate Disney into plot lines. So the shows, they own ABC. So ABC, stuff happens, they go to Disney. They own ESPN. What does everybody say when they win this? I'm going to Disney. So it's integrated on a broad level and then it's integrated in your own environment. You got, the kids got stuffed animals and videos. I got the D23 thing on the wall over here. Oh yeah, there's a reason they do, they have, I'm going to mess the number up. It's over 300,000 product skews at retail of Disney products. So there's dog dishes, there's utensils, there's galoshes, does anybody still say galoshes? There's coffee, there's, you name it. So you're going to have some Disney product around you if you're a Disney fan at all. And so the goal is the same. So if you look at GKIC and you clock a diamond member and you figure up every communication, non-sales communication and sales communication, you can separate them if you wish. If you just take the non-sales communications, it's 280 something a year. There's already 365 days. If you throw in the sales stuff, it's more like a couple thousand. So there's not, it's not a day going by that we're not like reminding you. If you walk into a good customer's office or house, you can't turn around 360 degrees without seeing stuff. They got all the Dan Kennedy books in one place on a shelf and they got Dan Kennedy bobbleheads sitting in front of it. And they got a cup and they got their audio program, their home study court, they got those over here. They got mouse pads, they got posters, they got stuff. Most people have in their offices, Grant Miller calls it as Dan Kennedy Shrine. It's two big bookcases full of everything. And so you're showing up broadly, books, webcasts, etc. You're showing up direct and you're showing up often, often, often, often. So what are the obvious benefits? Are there any other benefits to omnipresence other than just people are going to see you so they're going to refer? It affects referrals. In retention businesses, it affects retention. It can affect cumulative consumption. It can reflect, it can affect even satisfaction. How? Well, we're more willing to be a little tolerant with the foibles and mistakes of people that we are committed to, married to, in this daily ongoing relationship than we are with vendor in the same category with whom we are not very involved. And so it gives you a little room for flaws that now aren't fatal, that might be fatal otherwise. So this is what you call your top of content? Disney every so often aggravates me. But to disconnect, I mean I got a moment to dismantle the house. I got to change my whole life. And I refer people there all the time. So the emotional disconnect from all that. Whereas my dry cleaner, they screw up once. There's another one right next door. Absolutely. So what you're saying basically is you want to be on the top of people's consciousness. I want to move in. I mean I want to move in. I want to move in physically. I want to take up some space every day. And I want to take up space both physically and mentally. And I want to be part of their lives. People say they usually ask the staff right how do you get to be patient of the month anyway for which there is a script. Right. Well it's actually very easy. You keep all your appointments. You show up on time. You comply with the instructions for home care and you refer new patients. And by the way here's if we forgot to tell you here's how you refer new patients around here and here's the promotion of the month and here's some tools. Right. So the thank you reward. What you know when someone refers is you immediately know a couple of really important things. You know they can. You know they have the capability to do it because they just did it. Right. You know they will because they just did it. The likelihood of getting them to do that again versus getting somebody who's never done it to do it much easier to get the fifth one out of the person who can and will than the first one out of the person who maybe they can't or maybe they won't. So you make a big deal out of it. You get more. Right. Measurement and management. This whole thing has to be measured in management. How are we doing. How many new patients new customers new clients this month. How many referrals from them from this group the first month. How many did we get the second month. How many did we get the third month. Next month's group. How many. Right. Who has who hasn't. Who hasn't for six months. We got to do something about this. Right. They're they're not carrying their weight. Right. Staff. Staff if the staff won't refer from their circle of influence you have no hope of them motivating customers clients or patients to refer. So you got the status to refer. Direct conversation with non referring patient at some trigger point somebody has to sit with that patient that customer that client say Dave you know you've been with me for seven months you couldn't walk when you came in here you know and now you're back on the trapeze and and our average patient has brought us three patients by now.
